Announcements

The Freud Encyclopedia

Garland Press, Inc. is pleased to announce that work has begun on The Freud Encyclopedia: Theory, Therapy and Culture. The editors of the volume will be Edward Erwin, Michael Moscowitz, and James Walkup. Among the advisors are Adolf Grünbaum, Philip Holzman, and Theodore Shapiro.

The editors' goal is to present unbiased coverage, in alphabetical entries solicited from scholars around the world, of all aspects of Freud's life, theories, and influence.

Publication is scheduled for spring 1994. Inquiries should be directed to the Editor, Professor Edward Erwin, Department of Philosophy, University of Miami, Coral Gables, FL 33124.

Romanian Annotated Edition

The Editorial Committee of The Sigmund Freud Romanian Translation and Publication Trust Fund invites all interested analysts, scholars, and researchers to contribute introductory presentations to The Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud, Romanian Annotated Edition. Please address all requests for the guidelines and inquiries to: Coordinating Editor–The Sigmund Freud Romanian Translation and Publication Trust Fund, I Marine Midland Plaza, East Tower-Fourth Floor, Binghamton, NY 13901-3216.

He has most recently written Mapping the Mind (The Analytic Press, 1991), focusing on the need to integrate theories of mind and brain.

Psychoanalysis will always involve reexamining and revising its conception(s) of infant and childhood development. Recent infant observation studies, developments within psychoanalysis itself, and neuroscientific advances have forced the current reappraisals. Now older conflict and instinctual models are complemented by self psychology and object relations theories. Some contemporary theoretical positions—for example, those of Basch, Cohler, Demos, Emde, Fraiberg, Gedo, Goldberg, Kernberg, Kohut, Modell, Reiser, Sameroff, D. Stern, the Tolpins, and Wolf—have grown in stature as a result of the recent changes in psychoanalytic theory. So have some older contributions by pioneers such as Bowlby, Fairbairn, Anna Freud, Hartmann, I. Hendrick, Kestenberg, G. Klein, M. Klein, Pribram, Rapaport, Spitz, Tomkins, and Winnicott. Other contributions, notably Mahler's theory of separation–individuation, and work dependent on her assumptions of autistic and psychotic phases of development (i.e., the normal infant's supposed lack of self/object differentiation), have suffered significantly. Ultimately, time is unkind to all theories, but psychoanalysis as a science can only benefit from such ferment. To this progress we now add Lichtenberg's recognition of a number of decisive contributions and his worthwhile efforts to integrate these insights into psychoanalytic theory.

Lichtenberg's opus revolves around the core idea of five motivational “systems” (Sameroff, 1983). G. Klein's “The Vital Pleasures” (1974) represents an early precursor to such motivational systems conceptions, as does the work of Gedo and Goldberg (1973), and Gedo's (1986) more recent refinements on this theme. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Other Times, Other Realities

Toward a Theory of Psychoanalytic Treatment

Arnold H. Modell. Cambridge, MA and London, England: Harvard University Press, 1990, 180 pp., $27.50.

Ernest Wolf MD

Ernest Wolf

Chicago

Ernest Wolf   Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Northwestern University Medical School, Chicago; and Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty member of the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis. His interest has centered on the psychoanalytic psychology of the self and he is the author of Treating the Self: Elements of Clinical Self Psychology (Guilford, 1988).

Since the publication in 1968 of his first book, Object Love and Reality: An Introduction to a Psychoanalytic Theory of Object Relations, Arnold Modell has been recognized as an important and original contributor to psychoanalytic thought. Modell in that volume discussed the unsatisfactory state of theories of object relations in psychoanalysis. He lamented the lack of a unified theory, and, after touching on the contributions of many others but of Fairbairn and Winnicott in particular, presented his own original ideas in an elaboration that pointed toward a new synthesis. Now, more than 20 years later, Modell's focus of interest at first sight appears to have shifted towards psychoanalytic treatment. However, closer scrutiny of both title and contents reveals that he is still primarily concerned with theory, albeit now it is the theory of treatment leavened with a few case vignettes.

The book is addressed to the psychoanalytically sophisticated reader. Its introduction and 10 chapters take the reader through a history of ideas that have been postulated to explain why psychoanalysis works. Interspersed are valuable comments by Modell that include his own original contributions to the discussion.

As is customary in psychoanalysis, Modell begins by examining Freud's ideas about treatment. In contrast to Freud's metapsychology, which evolved and changed gradually under the impact of clinical observation, Modell calls Freud's theory of treatment incomplete and static. Freud took the psychoanalytic setting for granted as the frame within which one could apply the psychoanalytic technique.

The Primitive Edge of Experience

Thomas H. Ogden. Northvale, NJ: Aronson, 1989, 244 pp., $25.00.

Robert May PHD

Robert May

Robert May   Director of the Counseling and Mental Health Service at Amherst College. Trained at the Austen Riggs Center and the Tavistock Clinic, his interests include brief psychotherapy in a college setting, intensive psychoanalytic psychotherapy, and the British School of object relations.

I should begin by declaring my bias. I think Thomas Ogden is one of the most interesting and rewarding American writers about psychoanalytic theory and therapy. His earliest published piece (1976) is a minor gem in the literature of psychotherapy with university students. He went on to write two books (1982, 1986) which drew on his experience at the Tavistock Clinic and are, respectively, the best explication to date of the notion of projective identification and the best available description of the Kleinian viewpoint for an American audience. His writing is both straightforward and supple. At his best he has a wonderful capacity to weave theory and therapy together. In reading him you have the experience which superb teachers provide (the case conference comments of Erikson and Anna Freud come to mind): Theoretical concepts do not stand apart, waiting to be imposed on the patient, but rather the theory exists as the natural and inevitable framework for understanding a human struggle.

I do not intend to summarize the contents of Dr. Ogden's newest book. For those who want a summary there is the recent review in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis (King, 1990)—a review which gets the title of the book wrong, but gets the contents right enough. Instead, I wish to comment briefly on the structure and major concerns of the book and then, I hope, prepare an interested reader by touching on a few of the inevitable difficulties in reading this book and on some of what I think Ogden is up to.

I think this book can be confusing until one gets the framework clear. It naturally falls into several different pieces, linked, the publisher hopes, by the umbrella of the title, but in fact mainly linked by the author's orientation and point of view.

Kohut asked in what way an old unrealistic fantasy from childhood may be helpful in leading the individual forward to achievement and under what circumstances it may become a grave hindrance leading to blocked ambitions. Continuity and discontinuity are illustrated through comparison of a patient's two consecutive courses of psychotherapy, the first using drive theory and the second the concepts of self psychology demonstrating the restoration of continuity and the rekindling of the thrust to continue development.

Some Problems Created by Freud's Inconsistency

A Commentary on Reisner's Article

Robert Holt PHD

Robert Holt

Robert Holt   Professor of Psychology Emeritus, New York University. His most recent book is Freud Reappraised (Guilford, 1989). At his home in Truro, MA, he is working on an intellectual biography of Freud.

Many of the problems of Reisner's article are caused by his adopting an idiosyncratic definition of metapsychology, as Freud's theory of motivation. He is correct that Freud was (occasionally) aware of the interpersonal aspects of motives, but this useful insight is flawed by his tendentious misreadings in an attempt to document an extreme position. Freud's inconsistency aids the process. Several other errors in the article are also pointed out.

Loewenstein (1992) employs Lacan's rereading of the Dora case to argue that psychoanalytic life histories are multivocal, dialectical, and nonpositivistic. However, she neither acknowledges the problematic, but persistent, ties of psychoanalysis to positivism nor makes explicit important philosophical differences between Freud's and her own hermeneutical view. These deficiencies weaken her argument which thus does not provide sufficient grounds for rejecting Spence's view that Freud's case-reporting style is consistent with the Sherlock Holmes detective story genre.

The Quantitative Investigation of Psychoanalytic Theory and Therapy

A Bibliography 1986-1992

Dennis Shulman PHD

Dennis Shulman

Dennis Shulman   Senior member of the training faculty and a supervising analyst at the National Institute for the Psychotherapies, and Assistant Professor in Psychology at Fordham University's College at Lincoln Center. He has independent clinical practices in New York City and Bergen County, NJ. Dr. Shulman is President of the Society for the Advancement of Quantitative Research in Psychoanalysis and Editor of the Bulletin of the Psychoanalytic Research Society (the newsletter for Division 39's Section 6).

Psychoanalysis has been energetically criticized for its perceived failure to test empirically the critical hypotheses relating to its theory and therapy. Although the relationship between psychoanalysis and empirical research has been an uneasy one, more investigations of central aspects of the theory have been completed than is generally recognized. One of the major challenges experienced by the psychoanalyst/researcher involves the fact that psychoanalytically oriented research is either unpublished (doctoral dissertations or conference presentations) or scattered across a wide variety of journals, most often nonpsychoanalytic publications. In order to aid interested researchers and faculty teaching research seminars, and to stimulate discussion relating to psychoanalytic research methodology within the broader psychological community, a bibliography that includes more than 200 recent listings is offered.

She has recently published Freud, Dora, and Vienna 1900 (Free Press, 1991) and is currently at work on a comparative study of psychoanalysis in western Europe, concentrating on the distinctive intertwining of psychoanalysis and culture in each country.

In 1911, Sigmund Freud published a long paper about a German judge, Daniel Paul Schreber (1842-1911), who had been an inpatient in three psychiatric clinics from 1893 to 1902. A year after his release, Schreber (1903/1988) had published an account of his illness, and based on this memoir, Freud wrote at length on the subject of paranoia. Beginning in the 1940s and 1950s, psychiatrists and psychoanalysts began to give intensive attention to the life and illness of Schreber, an interest that has continued to the present day. In the 1970s, two authors, William G. Niederland (1974) and Morton Schatzman (1973), brought special notoriety to the role of Schreber's father in the illness of his son.

Now a Dutch sociologist, Han Israëls, has written a compelling and most unusual book about Schreber and his father. From one point of view it is a historical tour de force, bound to evoke admiration from any professional historian for its research and scholarship. From another vantage point, it is an example of historical overkill, a recitation of literally everything Israëls was able to find out about Judge Schreber and his family, but without bringing to bear the historian's usual attempts to organize and shape thematically his or her material.

What drove Israëls to try to discover everything he possibly could about Schreber and his family of origin? He hoped that information about Schreber's early life might shed some light on the deranged ideas of his later illness. This is an interest Israëls shares with many. [[RunningHead]]

A Self Psychology Perspective

The Protesting Patient

Helen Golden PHD

Helen Golden

Helen Golden   Director of Internship Training at Hillside Hospital/Long Island Jewish Medical Center, Supervisor and faculty member of the recent Einstein Postgraduate Psychotherapy Training Program, and currently on faculty at the Westchester Center for the Study of Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy. She is a past Treasurer of Division 39 and has been a member of its Board of Directors for the past 9 years.

I am very glad to have the opportunity to discuss the analysis of Dr. Dewald's patient from the viewpoint of self psychology. But before I do, I would like to state formally how gracious it is of Dr. Dewald to be willing to discuss a case of his that lies so far behind him now. I am sure I am not alone in believing that most of us would be hard put to do the same. But Dr. Dewald has for years set an admirable example of openness in the face of traditional psychoanalytic secrecy. His participation in this symposium affords us a rare chance to follow some of the ways that an experienced analyst's thought develops over the course of his career, to learn how he thinks about his past work now, and perhaps to hear something about the way he experiences his own development as a psychoanalyst over the 25 or more years since his contact with this patient. Revisionism abounds in the psychoanalytic literature, but the opportunity to go back in time to discuss, with its original author, an important case that received widespread attention at its time of publication, is rare, and Dr. Dewald certainly deserves our thanks for it.

Let me look first at the wider context in which my discussion is embedded. A study of this case is particularly evocative because its location in the landscape and history of psychoanalysis leads one to think also about how the spirit of psychoanalytic inquiry is handed down. It was published in 1972, 1 year after Kohut's (1971) seminal monograph, The Analysis of the Self, appeared. The authors were men of an age, and although Kohut's early education and medical training were in Vienna and Dewald's in this country, both matured as psychiatrists and psychoanalysts in the heartland of America. They both devoted much of their professional lives to teaching psychoanalysis, were deeply engaged with students, and as training analysts must have gained much of their clinical experience with very similar groups of analysands. Yet they came to very different end points.

Her research interests involve investigations of changes in the representational world in long-term psychoanalytically oriented treatment, and studies of linkages between the representational world and the world of family transactions. She is currently writing a book (with Jeri Doane, PhD) entitled Attachment and Emotional Climate in the Family: An Intergenerational Approach.

This article examines the pregnant therapist's experience of transference and countertransference as they are differentially expressed with male and female patients. Because pregnancy represents a concrete manifestation of the therapist's sexuality and fertility, it inevitably intensifies the erotic and maternal aspects of the transference. The different transferential configurations evoked by the therapist's pregnancy for male and female patients contain derivatives of early developmental issues around gestation and birth, and particularly the wish for a child and envy of female reproductive capacity. The divergent developmental pathways of the wish for a child are examined for males and females through a review of the psychoanalytic developmental literature. Case material is presented to illustrate that for both male and female patients, the therapist's pregnancy evokes procreative strivings that stem from a universal identification with the mother and envy of her generative power. However, whereas with female patients the recapitulation of childbearing wishes and their infantile forerunners consolidated female identification, with severely disturbed male patients these wishes precipitated a regressive identification with the mother's procreative functions, often expressed in pregenital terms, and an intensification of oedipal rivalries.

responseSet.title: The Freudian Case History: A Detective Story or a Dialectical Progression? Reflections on Psychoanalytic Narratives From a Lacanian Perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Freudian Case History

A Detective Story or a Dialectical Progression? Reflections on Psychoanalytic Narratives From a Lacanian Perspective

Era Loewenstein PHD

Era Loewenstein

Era Loewenstein   Supervisory Faculty of the Department of Psychiatry at California Pacific Medical Center and on the Teaching Faculty at Mt. Zion Hospital, San Francisco. She is in private practice in San Francisco. Her interests include the psychoanalytic process, the structure of subjectivity, and the narrative dimension of the psychoanalytic process.

In response to the recent tendency to discard Freud's contributions as anachronistic and reductionistic this article critically examines Donald Spence's contention that the Freudian style of case reporting is ensnared in an outdated positivistic tradition. Spence, I argue, mistakenly reduces Freud's method of historical construction to a form of naive commitment to the Sherlock Holmes detective story genre. By employing Lacan's rereading of Dora's case history, I conversely demonstrate that in the psychoanalytic situation the analysand's past unfolds in a series of dialectical reversals yielding not a linear, detective-like story with a single solution, but an ever-unfolding and subversive narrative, a life story which stubbornly resists premature closure.

responseSet.title: Narcissism, Shame, Masochism, and Object Relations: An Exploratory Correlational Study
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcissism, Shame, Masochism, and Object Relations

An Exploratory Correlational Study

Stephen Hibbard PHD

Stephen Hibbard

Knoxville

Stephen Hibbard   Advanced graduate student in clinical psychology at the University of Tennessee, a member of the Appalachian Psychoanalytic Society and of the Graduate Student Board of Division 39. He holds a doctoral degree in Philosophy from the University of California, Davis. Chief research interests are narcissism, object relations, unconscious processes, the interface between cognitive psychology and psychoanalysis, theoretical psychology, and film semiotics.

A correlational study (N = 701) examined three measures of narcissism, a measure of shame, two measures of masochism, a measure of object relations, and a measure of social desirability. Moderate correlations were found for a core of constructs which have been described in the clinical and theoretical literature. These are narcissism, shame, object relations, and masochism. Narcissism seemed to divide into two different styles, a “phallic,” grandiose style and a narcissistically vulnerable style. Shame primarily accounted for the differences in these styles, correlating negatively with the grandiose style, positively with the more vulnerable style. The narcissistically vulnerable style correlated more with the core pathology measures; that is, object relations and masochism. Social desirability did not mediate the relationship between grandiose narcissism and shame. Only small univariate gender differences were found, but masochism was a better predictor of shame in women than was narcissism whereas there was little difference between masochism and narcissism for predicting shame in men.

A Structural Perspective on Analytic Activity

Alan Sugarman PHD

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman   Senior instructor at the San Diego Psychoanalytic Society and Institute and a Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry at the University of California, San Diego. He is coeditor of the volumes Borderline Phenomena and the Rorschach Test and The Technique and Practice of Psychoanalysis, Volume II: A Memorial Volume to Ralph R. Greenson. Current scholarly interests include elaborating the technical implications of the structural model as well as applying psychoanalytic developmental understanding to diagnostic and analytic practice.

Recent debates about analytic strategy revolve around different stances by the analyst in regard to his or her activity. Proponents of a relational stance often misunderstand classical analytic technique and assume that classical analysts are cold, detached, or critical. Modern-day classical thinking on technique is discussed in an effort to correct this misunderstanding. Two clinical vignettes from analyses of adolescents are presented to demonstrate modern-day classical technique. These vignettes show that a classical analyst can remain neutral without being cold, detached, or critical so long as an emphasis on understanding and interpreting intrapsychic conflict is maintained.

Narcissism and Parenting Styles

P. Watson PHD , Tracy Little BS and Michael Biderman PHD

Michael Biderman

Michael Biderman   Professor of Psychology at the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga. While conducting research in many areas, his principal concerns are in the application of current statistical methods to problems in Industrial/Organizational psychology and in the development of mathematical models for simple decision processes.

Baumrind's authoritative, permissive, and authoritarian parenting styles were analyzed within the context of Kohut's psychology of the self. College student perceptions of their parents were correlated with measures of self-functioning in order to test the hypotheses that perceived parental authoritativeness would be associated with less narcissistic maladjustment, that permissiveness would be associated with immature grandiosity, and that authoritarianism would correlate with inadequate idealization. All three suggestions received empirical support. In addition, authoritarianism seemed to be associated with less immature grandiosity, and parenting characteristics of the mother were more strongly correlated with self-development than were those of the father.

The Emergence of the Transference Storm in the Psychoanalytic Process

William Young PHD

William Young

William Young   Professor of Psychology at the California Graduate Institute. He is also a Training and Supervising Analyst with the school's Psychoanalytic Department and at the Newport Psychoanalytic Institute. His research and publications began with his association with the Postgraduate Center for Mental Health, NY and continues to take both a clinical and theoretical focus. His recent interests include the use of native phenomenology as a beginning point for psychoanalytic inquiry, the integration of diverse scholarly disciplines to support this approach, and the investigation of psychoanalytic process.

The metaphor of a storm is identified as an emotionally compelling image frequently used in literary productions throughout history. It is proposed that a careful examination of the structural properties of the storm metaphor and their correspondences with the therapeutic process will illuminate the emotional nature of change in the transference relationship. As such, the transference storm is a process concept. It denotes the progressive character of derivative manifestations in treatment. It offers a means of recognizing the significance of events leading to the emergence of acute transference feelings. It is offered as an initial and prototypical concept for developing clinical process theory. Finally, in this respect, it provides for a description of the unfolding of analytic investigation; and it does so without the constraint of theoretical or diagnostic specificity, while avoiding the vagueness of eclectic approaches.

Conscious and Unconscious

Freud's Dynamic Distinction Reconsidered, Patricia S. Herzog. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1991, ix + 118 pp., $27.50.

Nigel Mackay DPhil

Nigel Mackay

Nigel Mackay   Lecturer in Psychology at the University of Wollongong, New South Wales, Australia, where he teaches on the Postgraduate Program in Clinical Psychology, and in the history and metatheory of psychology. His research interests are largely in metatheoretical problems in psychology, clinical psychology, and psychoanalysis. His most recent major work in the area is Motivation and Explanation: An Essay on Freud's Philosophy of Science (Psychological Issues Monograph 56, International Universities Press, 1989).

There is a sense in which all of Freud's theorizing about mind is an attempt to make sense of a simple but powerful fact: There are states of mind in which the features that characterize a person's normal, conscious, and self-centered mentality are absent, yet somehow these other states of mind are a part of that person and are involved in the person's actions, thoughts, and of course psychopathology. The successive formulations of Freud's theories, from the pretopographical through the neuropsychological Project and the topographical to the structural, each attempt to articulate this fact in a conceptually coherent form, compatible with the clinical data, other psychological theories, and the materialist metatheory with which Freud operated. Through these successive formulations it is the conscious/unconscious distinction in its various forms—descriptive, systematic, but especially dynamic—which bears the main theoretical load.

Despite its importance, Freud never felt that he gave an account of the relation of the conscious to the unconscious which was complete and satisfied his metatheoretical criteria for psychological theory: That it be bio-evolutionarily justifiable, conceptually coherent, and in accord with the clinical data. And the reconceptualizations of this relation which psychoanalysts have attempted ever since show that others have not been satisfied with the matter either.

Patricia Herzog's book is a critical examination of the way in which Freud presented the conscious/unconscious distinction.

Dimensions of Psychoanalysis

Joseph Sandler. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1989, xv + 263 pp., $30.00.

John Gedo MD

John Gedo

Chicago

John Gedo   After 30 years as a psychoanalyst, has dropped his institutional affiliations. He has written or edited a dozen books, most recently The Biology of Clinical Encounters (The Analytic Press, 1991). He is currently coediting a volume on the concept of hierarchies in psychoanalytic theory.

At present, there are two endowed chairs of psychoanalysis in the world, the Freud Professorships at the Hebrew University (Jerusalem) and at the University College (London). Perhaps it has not been possible to establish a similar position in an American university because in this country psychoanalysis has gradually narrowed to a clinical discipline, relegated to the back rooms of medical schools and training programs in psychology devoted to the production of practitioners.

I have been stimulated to express these bilious sentiments by the recent publication of the man who has held both Freud Professorships, Joseph Sandler. Dimensions of Psychoanalysis is a collection of Inaugural Lectures by the sequence of scholars who held the Freud chair in London while it was assigned to visiting dignitaries. It is remarkable that a substantial minority of the dozen lecturers chose non-clinical topics, and two Freud Professors were not professional psychoanalysts at all.

The selection of visiting professors was, of course, dictated by their availability for such an assignment: It causes no surprise that most of them practice psychoanalysis in London or Paris. By contrast, the four American participants were all academics on sabbatical leave. The invited British analysts represent the leadership of several factions within the London psychoanalytic community, but the French and the American visitors seem to have been chosen on a less adventuresome basis.

Although Sandler provides the volume with an excellent Introduction (where he expertly summarizes the thesis of each contribution), he fails to indicate whether the Lectures as published reflect the current thinking of the authors or their views at the time they were at University College.

The tension between remembering and forgetting is the daily experience of the psychoanalyst. This takes place not only in the consulting room, but applies to our sense of ourselves and the history of psychoanalysis. Anna Freud died in October 1982. For almost 60 years she had been the heir apparent and then the leader of the international psychoanalytic movement. Yet, not even 10 years after her death, her name seems to have disappeared from psychoanalytic discourse and the contributions of her work and of child analysis to the body of psychoanalytic theory and technique are not discussed. Rudolf Ekstein, in his touching preface to Peter Heller's book, stresses the importance of knowing our psychoanalytic history, which tends to be ignored because “in our pragmatic culture many students are only interested in the immediate task, the instant recipe that is to help them meet a situation, change a symptom, and so on” (p. xv). Despite the proliferation of new psychoanalytic trainings, Ekstein's point is reflected in their tendency to omit a child analytic component. Thus it seems that many have forgotten the crucial contributions of child analysis in the evolution of our field.

The tendency to forget is countered by the urge to remember; recently a number of books have appeared about Anna Freud and her generation of psychoanalysts—what Katherine Rees (1991) has called “our Bloomsbury group.” Peter Heller's book, A Child Analysis with Anna Freud (originally published in German, in 1983)

Arnold Tobin   Training and supervising analyst at Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis. He is interested in research on the psychoanalytic process and dreams, particularly works in the area of applying psychoanalytic ideas to social problems such as criminality, addictions, and perversions.

Here we have a monumental book about a very significant person, especially to those of us in Chicago. Dr. Weiss has done a remarkable job in selecting some of the most significant articles by Joan Fleming on the issue that also is the title of the book. In addition he has included an excellent forward by Robert Wallerstein, who knew Dr. Fleming's work and ideas very well, and a fine epilogue of his own based on his experiences with Fleming in this area. The articles include one by Fleming and Benedek and four which the editor wrote with Fleming. There are also four articles written exclusively by Fleming.

By reading this book, one has an opportunity to rework one's ideas about what it means to become a psychoanalyst. It begins with the nature of analytic learning, the concept of self-analysis, and the central features of being a psychoanalyst. Then it goes on to trace the steps in the training process. Fleming integrated all of these steps and she saw them as forming a cohesive whole. That is, she saw the didactic and clinical aspects as being interrelated, and she identified, very carefully and specifically, the interface between the didactic and clinical and supervisory experiences and the training analysis. The book includes specific case material relating to the issues of selection and matriculation that demonstrates very clearly how Fleming felt the Chicago Institute should go about these processes: Not only what to look for in the candidate but even how to go about evaluating the candidate or potential candidate. There are also excellent clinical examples of supervision. Finally, she goes on to the training of the supervisor himself or herself.

[[RunningHead]]

Motivation and Explanation

An Essay on Freud's Philosophy of Science, by Nigel Mackay. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1989, x + 254 pp., $27.50.

Donald Spence PHD

Donald Spence

Donald Spence   Professor of Psychiatry at the Robert Wood Johnson Medical School, University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey. He is the author of Narrative Truth and Historical Truth and The Freudian Metaphor. He is currently completing a book with the working title of The Rhetorical Voice of Psychoanalysis.

The book under review is not only an essay on Freud's philosophy of science (as the subtitle has it) but more particularly, a determined attack on the “separate-domain” thesis. This thesis asserts that psychoanalysis belongs to “a domain of explanation separate from explanations of nonhuman phenomena” (p. 179). In refuting this claim, Mackay argues that psychoanalysis falls clearly within the domain of normal science and, by implication, deserves all the rights and privileges of other established disciplines. We hear the echo of Freud when he wrote that “I have always felt it as a gross injustice that people have refused to treat psycho-analysis like any other science” (1925, p. 58).

Mackay tells us that early in his career, he went to hear a lecture by Roy Schafer on the split between the clinical data and metapsychological theory and came away “convinced [that] his thesis was wrong” (p. 1). He found that Schafer's lecture “concentrated my mind wonderfully” and, as a consequence, conceived the idea for the present book. We can hear the allusion to Samuel Johnson's well-known description of what happens to the prisoner when he knows that he will be hanged within the fortnight.

If the separate-domain thesis turns out to be true, does that represent the death (by hanging) of psychoanalysis? Many of the participants in this debate behave as if this were the likely outcome. Beginning with Blight's rhetorical question in 1981, “Must psychoanalysis retreat to hermeneutics?” (Blight, 1981), and coming down to the present time, the argument between those who see psychoanalysis as a natural science and those who see it as something else has become increasingly polarized, shrill, and combative, and the dimensions of a shared middle ground have grown steadily smaller.

W. Meissner

W. Meissner   Training and Supervising Analyst at the Boston Psychoanalytic Institute and University Professor of Psychoanalysis at Boston College. His research interests are in psychoanalysis, psychotherapy, psychobiography, and the relation between religion and psychoanalysis.

The present volume is a hardcover edition of a previous supplement to the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association based on a series of panels presented at meetings of the American on the role of the concept of structure in current psychoanalytic theory and practice. The result can be taken as a more or less authoritative expression of current thinking about structure in orthodox analytic circles. As such, the volume is informative and in many regards thought provoking. But if readers are looking for a coherent, consistent, and consensually endorsed presentation of the idea of structure, or for a uniform conceptualization of the nature of psychic structure and its role in psychoanalytic praxis, they will have to look elsewhere. The volume is more remarkable for its diversity and often contradictory views on the nature of structure and the relevance of structure to clinical work in the analytic setting.

One would expect the concept of psychic structure to be a basic element of psychoanalytic theory to which all quasi-orthodox analysts would nod assent. But it seems that espousing a structural concept is quite a different matter from determining what is meant by structure and how it is to be interpreted in its divergent theoretical contexts. If the Freudian version of the structural theory—in the form of the tripartite model—seemed clear enough for earlier analytic thinkers, that claim can no longer be sustained. There is no consensus in this collection of essays on that issue. One can note as well a certain dissatisfaction with the traditional structural ideas, possibly linked to outmoded drive concepts as noted by Schafer. The result seems to be an allergic withdrawal from theory, or at least an attempt to get along with as little theory as needed to conduct analyses.

Introduction

Gilead Nachmani PHD

Gilead Nachmani

Gilead Nachmani   Training and Supervising Analyst, and on the teaching faculty of the William Alanson White Institute. He is a Clinical Professor of Psychology and Supervisor, Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy at Adelphi University, and a Supervisor of Psychotherapy in the Doctoral Program in Clinical Psychology at City University of New York.

Is there an epistemology of psychoanalytic cure? Does one particular method of relatedness represent the “only” avenue through which cure is realized? Do other psychoanalytic schools, with their differing theories of human development, conflict, analytic method, and outcome, lend themselves to systematically criticize an already extant case? Among the limitations underscored by such trans-theoretical challenges are the different definitions of what constitutes relatedness, transference, “the human condition,” the goals, and the expected outcomes of psychoanalyses. Can the working assumptions of one psychoanalytic orientation be meaningful and usable to another? Likewise, can the outcomes expected by one methodology be considered as expectable by another, needless to say comprehensible? Critics do not view a case presentation only from different theoretical perspectives. They view clinical data from perspectives of personal values, as well as what has worked for them.

With the possible benefit of hindsight and the assuredness of theoretical fundamentalism, the three contributors to this discussion consider an already concluded 2-year analysis; in particular, the material of its 15th month. The analysis, presented with candor in rich explanative classical psychoanalytic detail by Dr. Paul Dewald, was published 20 years ago in 1972. The case, according to Dewald, was one of “classical hysterical and oedipal conflict with the major psychopathology revolving around [the patient's] relationship to her father and her sexual conflicts around this” (p. 14).

Author's Discussion

Paul Dewald MD

Paul Dewald

Paul Dewald   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, St. Louis University School of Medicine, and Training and Supervising Analyst at the St. Louis Psychoanalytic Institute. He is in private practice in St. Louis and is the author of three widely known books, seven book chapters, and over 30 articles in the field of psychoanalysis and psychiatry.

I begin by thanking the symposium chair and the symposium members for discussing my book. This was one of the aims I had in writing it, and I am deeply grateful to the participants whose careful, systematic, and scholarly reading of the book contributed so much to the discussion. The analysis took place between 1963 and 1965. I had completed my analytic training in 1961. I had not yet published an analytic paper and was relatively unknown when I moved to St. Louis in 1963 and became chairman of the clinic committee. At that time in St. Louis, we had an affiliation with the Chicago Institute as a geographic extension. All clinic cases had to be presented to the Chicago Clinic Committee for approval, and that is where this case was eventually turned down. I still thought it was a good case, and the husband's willingness to work two jobs to support the patient's analysis, the availability of a loan from his parents, and other financial arrangements made possible what previously would have been a major sacrifice.

In 1963, my practice was to take extensive process notes on each patient that I saw. My feeling, at that time, was that our field was deeply limited by our lack of primary data and we needed to begin to collect data. I have file cabinets full of notebooks of data, most of which, unfortunately, are unusable or unused because the material is either too voluminous, too vague, or too undramatic in its presentation. The idea of presenting this patient in book form occurred to me about two thirds of the way through the analysis. I became aware that there had been some rather rapid and profound changes taking place, and I felt that it might be possible as an analogy to the process of phase cinematography to show the evolving process in a more dramatic form. In that sense, this patient's case seemed to me to be ideal.

On the other hand, in order not to do anything to destroy this potential, I did not review the material as I received it. I did not begin to write the book or to conceptualize it. Two years after the termination, I wrote to the patient. I asked—again, with no rereading of the data—whether she would consider a follow-up interview.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Mid-Life Crisis

Peter Newton PHD

Peter Newton

Peter Newton   Professor of Psychology at The Wright Institute in Berkeley, CA. His research has been in the areas of group dynamics, psychoanalytic treatment, and adult development. His developmental biography of Freud is being published by Guilford.

Freud's youth had been animated by a Dream of great scientific discovery. By the onset of middle age, he had not yet succeeded in realizing it, and his efforts to do so intensified. Between the ages of 39 and 44, Freud first tried to create a neuronal psychology, then focused his efforts on finding the origins of neurosis. With the seduction theory, Freud thought he had resolved the problem of etiology, as well as secured the means for the realization of his Dream. However, he was unable to suppress recurring doubts about the theory, and his inability to do so precipitated a mid-life developmental crisis. Unable either to settle his mind about seduction or to solve the problem of neurosis along other lines, he reluctantly turned to The Interpretation of Dreams in order to pull himself out of the crisis.

Reshaping his Dream from a narrowly masculine, ‘hard’ science to a broader, more feminine, ‘soft’ science version required considerable mid-life individuation, including further integration of his own femininity. As Freud grew comfortable with a more feminine subject matter and a more feminine way of working, he was able to see that his work on The Interpretation of Dreams would also illuminate the problem of neurosis, albeit in purely psychodynamic terms. By the conclusion of his work on the book, Freud had created a new version of his Dream suitable for middle age; he would discover the relations, not between neuron and neurosis, but between conflict and life—clinical psychology, as he called it.

A Note on Deep-Seated Social Values and Countertransference in Mother-Infant Dyadic Psychotherapy

Eva Gochman PHD

Eva Gochman

Parent and Infant Development Program
Washington, DC

Eva Gochman   Director of the Parent and Infant Development Program of the Commission on Mental Health Services, managed by the Government of the District of Columbia. Her interest in development and prevention of psychopathology led her to conceptualize psychotherapeutic interventions for the mother-infant dyad and consequently to create the Parent and Infant Development Program. Her current intellectual interests center on changes in the model as applied to different diagnostic categories, such as substance abusers, and also concern issues in clinical supervision.

Social values shared by most cultures, and therefore shared by therapist and client, can easily elude conscious awareness. Reflected in countertransference, this can interfere with the therapy process. An example is countertransference in mother-infant dyadic psychotherapy based in the value: “A mother takes care of her child.” Once this deep-seated social value is brought to conscious awareness, the interfering nature of this countertransference is recognized. The therapist is freed to work with the mother toward her underlying treatment goal, which is to “be a good mother.” If she is not emotionally capable of being the child's primary caretaker, the objective of enhanced attachment can be abandoned and “being a good mother” can be translated into “allowing the child to have a competent caretaker.” The treatment objective can become the mother's emotional separation from her infant. Without this clarification of the social values bound in the countertransference, the therapist would continue to work at cross-purposes with the dyad.

Breuer and Freud ended their relationship allegedly because Breuer objected to Freud's claim that there was a sexual etiology for the psychoneuroses. The unconscious link between sexuality and intellection remains as problematic for us today as it was 100 years ago.

Patrick J. Casement. New York: Guilford, xiv + 386 pp., $35.00.

Arnold Modell MD

Arnold Modell

Arnold Modell   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at the Harvard Medical School and a Training and Supervising Analyst at the Boston Psychoanalytic Institute. He is also a visiting fellow at the Neurosciences Institute at The Rockefeller University. Dr. Modell is completing a book entitled The Private Self in Public Space. His current interests include an attempt to find points of convergence between psychoanalytic theory and the biology of the brain.

Learning From the Patient is intended as a textbook on the technique of psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic therapy for beginning students and supervisors. The fact that therapist and supervisor are paired in this fashion already points to the unusual nature of this book. Casement assumes that there is a certain symmetry between the therapeutic and the supervisory relationships in that they are both communicative relationships in which the unconscious of one participant interacts with that of the other. I believe that we have not given sufficient attention to the extraordinary fact that we can learn of the psychic reality of our student' patient even though that patient' psychic reality may be outside of the conscious awareness of our student. In line with this symmetry between the therapeutic and supervisory relationship, Casement believes that it is also necessary to provide a holding environment for one' student. Further, he assumes a certain equivalence between supervising another and supervising oneself. For, instead of speaking of self-analysis he speaks of the internal supervisor.

Learning From the Patient is strongly influenced by the contributions of Bion and Winnicott. These men shared a certain egalitarian attitude with regard to the patient and both appreciated the pervasiveness of paradox in the therapeutic encounter. Casement bases his approach to technique on Winnicott' advice to follow the patient' lead. It is the patient who decides how to make use of the therapist and not the other way around. This egalitarian attitude enables one to examine one' countertransference without prejudice, for it is always an open question as to whose pathology it is. The patient may learn from the therapist but perhaps in even greater measure the therapist learns from the patient.

Behavioral Theories of Depression

Sidney Blatt PHD and Celine Maroudas BSc

Celine Maroudas

Celine Maroudas   Did her undergraduate work at Cambridge University, England and is a graduate student in the Department of Psychology at The Hebrew University of Jerusalem, with primary interests in child clinical psychology.

This article reviews three contemporary psychoanalytic theories that articulate two types of depression, based on disruptions (a) of interpersonal relatedness or (b) of self-esteem, and that consider the nature, etiology, and treatment of depression based on the phenomenology of the issues that cause individuals to become depressed. The work of Arieti and Bemporad from an interpersonal perspective, of Bowlby from an ethological and object relations perspective, and of Blatt from the perspective of psychoanalytic ego psychology and cognitive developmental theory, are compared and contrasted with each other and with recent formulations of Beck, who approaches this topic from a cognitive–behavioral perspective. This article reviews these four theoretical positions with the goal of deriving a unified theory of depression that has implications for the treatment process. Areas of agreement and overlap as well as disagreements and differences among these four theoretical positions are articulated and discussed particularly in terms of their implications for treatment. In addition, suggestions are made about possible investigations that should be directed toward resolving some of the divergences and contradictions.

It is generally believed that a treatment that is more effective than its alternatives will be used, but psychological treatments for schizophrenic and other psychotic reactions have been avoided despite the evidence for their effectiveness from the time of “moral treatment” to the present. Less effective (or even destructive) treatments have been seized upon, in part because they do not require understanding these patients. Understanding schizophrenic persons means facing facts about ourselves, our families, and our society that we do not want to know, or to know again (in the case of repressed feelings and experiences). The central role of terror in producing symptoms, and the genesis and psychological handling of symptoms, including delusions and hallucinations, are briefly described.

William McGrath PHD

William McGrath

William McGrath   Professor of History at the University of Rochester, and has written on topics concerning 19th and 20th century European intellectual history. He is the author of Dionysian Art and Populist Politics in Austria and Freud's Discovery of Psychoanalysis: The Politics of Hysteria.

Within the broadly defined goal of investigating the social origins of psychoanalysis, Peter Homans' book, The Ability to Mourn: Disillusionment and the Social Origins of Psychoanalysis, undertakes a series of strikingly original and thought-provoking explorations into the history of the psychoanalytic movement, its place in the traditions of Western culture, and its possible role in defining a more satisfactory relationship to modernity. In addition to providing a sociological study of one of the most influential movements of our time, the book also attempts to put forward a new psychoanalytic theory of culture capable of overcoming the limitations of Freud' cultural theories. It is rare indeed that a scholarly work sets for itself such ambitious goals, and in grappling with them the book yields a wealth of valuable insights.

The book is divided into three parts, the first two of which are devoted primarily to the origins and early development of psychoanalysis while the third takes up the contemporary cultural significance of psychoanalysis and Homans' own theory of culture. The underlying thesis of the first two parts of the book is that psychoanalysis arose from a centuries-long process of mourning dating as far back as the 14th century. The term usually employed to describe this process is secularization, but the author argues that the concept of mourning is more useful in that it allows him to get at the personal psychodynamic forces involved in the process, whereas the concept of secularization does not. As Homans notes (p. 4), in “Mourning and Melancholia” Freud indicated that mourning is an appropriate response not just to the loss of a loved person, but also to “the loss of some abstraction which has taken the place of one, such as one' country, liberty, an ideal and so on.

This commentary addresses the controversy concerning session frequency in psychoanalysis. We argue that in most analyses, an increase in frequency will lead to qualitative changes in the data obtained and in the ways each person can use the material. The analysand should generate more associations which focus on the dyadic here and now. Each participant should better understand the other's personalized use of language. More frequent sessions should increase the analysand's sense of the “holding environment” and make a deeper therapeutic regression more likely. It should also reduce the analyst's press to intervene or introduce “parameters.” Efforts to help the analysand deal with his or her shame and acquire missing psychological skills also seem more likely in a maximally intensive therapy.

Freud and the Intentionality of Affect

Jerome Wakefield DSW

Jerome Wakefield

Jerome Wakefield   Assistant Professor at the Columbia University School of Social Work, and Postdoctoral Fellow at the Institute for Health, Health Care Policy, and Aging Research, Rutgers University. His main interests are in the conceptual foundations of psychology. Topics of current research include the logical reconstruction of Freudian theory, the concept of mental disorder, the relations of ancient Greek philosophy to contemporary theories of psychotherapy, the history of sexuality, and the nature of altruism.

I argue that Freud implicitly adopted Brentano's thesis that the essence of the mental is intentionality (i.e., mental representation), while rejecting Brentano's Cartesian assumption that intentionality must be conscious. But, how can a feeling like free-floating anxiety, which does not seem to represent or be about anything, be fitted into Freud's representational framework? Several possible answers are examined, including: (a) affects are ideas, (b) affects are always attached to ideas, (c) consciousness is perception of internal mental states, and (d) affects are perceptions of internal bodily processes. I argue that only the “bodily perception” account is systematically developed by Freud, is consistent with Freud's other doctrines, and is intrinsically plausible even in the context of contemporary debate. Freud's intentionalistic account of affect is shown to be part of an overall explanation of Freud's paradoxical rejection of unconscious affects.

Robert Sokolove   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychology and Psychiatry at Boston University. His ongoing programs of research include investigating the relationship of normative personality dimensions in personality disorders, and the relationship of neurobiological to social variables in schizophrenia.

Psychoanalytically oriented theorists have repeatedly reported observing in patients diagnosed with borderline personality disorder severe pathology in the capacity to utilize internalized imagery as a source of self-soothing. Empirical support was provided for this observation: Borderline patients demonstrated a developmentally lower capacity for object representation and evocative memory of affective object relationships, fewer positively toned representations, and a more pervasive experience of aloneness than patients with neurotic character pathology. Moreover, the experience of aloneness was the strongest clinical predictor of diagnosis, supporting the contention that inner emptiness or aloneness may be central to these patients' subjective experience.

His current interests focus on the application of object relations theory and self psychology to the treatment situation.

Dr. Dewald's (1972) case presentation in The Psychoanalytic Process is an extraordinary contribution to the field of psychoanalysis. It is among the most detailed, extensive, and clearly presented cases of psychoanalysis in the literature and represents an effort of great personal courage and a commitment to intellectual and scientific exploration. Dr. Dewald as a senior analyst has a clear understanding of classical psychoanalytic theory and technique as well as a comprehensive sense of himself as a member of the psychoanalytic dyad. This text provides us with an opportunity to compare the efficacy of different theoretical and technical approaches to psychoanalysis. Criticism and revision have marked the history of psychoanalysis; an early example involved Freud's comments to Binswanger (1963) in which he generously wrote that his own work would serve as the foundation for the Psychoanalytic house, which would continue to be built. I want to underscore my appreciation and gratitude for Dr. Dewald's efforts and I hope that our work will stand as an addition to the house of Psychoanalysis.

In this article I focus on how the analyst's theoretical system influences his understanding of the patient's symptoms and dynamics as well as how theory structures the interpretive process and the goals of treatment. I present an interpretation of the patient's dynamics from the perspective of British object relations theory and suggest how this approach would structure a different therapeutic experience. My discussion emphasizes the patient's failure at mastering the anxieties of the paranoid position and her related difficulties in not being able to consolidate a separate and autonomous identity as an adult woman.

Some Problems Created by Holt' Consistency

A Rejoinder

Steven Reisner PHD

Steven Reisner

New York

Steven Reisner   Director of Psychology at the Regent Hospital, New York and Adjunct Assistant Professor in Psychology and Education at Teachers College, Columbia University, where he teaches a seminar in the development of Freud's thought.

Holt' critique of my article is based on fundamental differences in our approach to Freud studies and its relevance to contemporary psychoanalytic theory and practice. In my rejoinder I argue that Holt' view of Freud' metapsychology is biased along two dimensions: He accepts the narrowed scientistic view of Freud' metapsychology which is the legacy of Freud' followers and translators, and he asserts empiricist criteria for evaluating that metapsychology which are the legacy of American ego-psychology. As a result he underemphasizes the relational, hermeneutic, and epistemological components of Freud' theory. I argue that Holt' criteria are not the best for evaluating the metapsychology or judging which aspects of Freud' theory are most helpful to contemporary psychoanalysis.

Homophobia and the Psychologist's Role in Psychoanalytic Training Institutes

David Flaks JD

David Flaks

David Flaks   Attorney and a doctoral student at Widener University's Institute for Graduate Clinical Psychology where his interests include psychoanalytic psychotherapy with gay and lesbian clients. His dissertation research, “Lesbian Mothers and Their Children: Psychological and Legal Implications,” received a 1991 American Psychological Association Dissertation Research Award. He also received a 1992 Distinguished Student Contribution Award from Division 44.

In 1988, a settlement agreement in a federal antitrust suit secured for psychologists the right to apply for training with the American Psychoanalytic Association (the American). It is the premise of this article that this victory for psychologists also confronted them with an ethical dilemma because the American has a history of discrimination against gay men and lesbians. This article explores the ethical predicament facing psychologists who wish to train as psychoanalysts. It concludes that it is incumbent upon them to be vigilant against future homophobia within the American and actively voice opposition to discriminatory attitudes or actions.

The Primitive Personality and the Organic Personality

A Neuropsychodynamic Model for Evaluation and Treatment

Laurence Miller PHD

Laurence Miller

Boca Raton, FL

Laurence Miller   Private practice in neuropsychology and psychotherapy in Boca Raton, FL. His clinical and research interests include the integration of neuropsychological and psychodynamic approaches to personality and psychotherapy. He lectures and writes widely on topics pertaining to the brain, behavior, health, and society, and is the author of Inner Natures (St. Martin's) and Freud's Brain (Guilford).

Although traditionally seen as disparate clinical entities, primitive personalities and organic personalities share many diagnostic and therapeutic characteristics. Both involve disruptions in the core self-system, and both require corrective-educative psychotherapeutic techniques, in addition to more interpretive ones. A previously well-adjusted personality may be rendered somewhat more “primitive” by brain damage, while an impulsive, emotionally labile, nonreflective primitive personality may place himself or herself at increased risk for acquired injury to an already-vulnerable brain. Suggestions for evaluation and treatment of these patients are offered.

This article presents a study of 25 children analyzed at the Anna Freud Centre who physically attacked their analyst in the course of treatment, requiring restrictions in the form of physical restraint (holding the child) along with verbal intervention and interpretation. Of the cases studied, two primary groups were found: (a) those who struggled with separation anxiety and the fear of abandonment, and (b) those who had developed a sadomasochistic mode of relating which was reenacted in the course of the analysis. The discussion of the two groups includes technical findings and recommendations.

Freud's Narrow Escape and the Discovery of Transference

Ann Salyard EDD

Ann Salyard

Haverford, PA

Ann Salyard   Educational Counselor and Consultant in Haverford, PA. Her current research and writing focuses on the origins of psychoanalysis. Her interests include psychoanalysis as a theory of intellectual development, the influence of college education on adult intellectual development, and the study of teacher—student interaction.

In this article I contend that Freud discovered transference in connection with material derived from his treatment of Emma Eckstein. The last chapter of Studies on Hysteria (Breuer & Freud, 1895), in which Freud's first published use of the term transference occurs, can be read as a working through of the crisis which occurred when Eckstein nearly died. This new concept, it is argued, explained his patient's disturbed feelings toward him as a “false connection” and thereby helped to free him of feelings of personal involvement in her libidinal demands. The story of the troubled circumstances under which Freud discovered transference provides insight into the defensive nature of the concept, Freud's delay in writing on the subject, the paucity of materials he produced on it, and the fact that in his own work, Freud did not give transference the central attention it deserved. It also suggests how Freud's personal experience contributed to this major discovery.

[[RunningHead]]

Supervision in Relationship

Resolving the Teach— Treat Controversy in Psychoanalytic Supervision

Joan Sarnat PHD

Joan Sarnat

Joan Sarnat   Senior Associate Faculty Member in the Department of Clinical Psychology at Antioch/New England Graduate School in Keene, NH, and maintains a private practice of psychodynamic psychotherapy and supervision in Amherst, MA. She is currently interested in relational, feminist, and postmodern thinking about the treatment process, supervision, and graduate training.

Much of the recent literature on psychoanalytically oriented supervision assumes that the effective supervisor must at times address supervisee conflicts and relationship problems that interfere with role functioning. However, a vocal minority continues to assert that such a supervisory stance moves beyond teaching into “treating” the supervisee and is therefore inappropriate and potentially destructive. I consider the basis for this objection, and suggest a means of addressing it. I argue that explicit acknowledgment that the supervisor is a participant-observer with anxieties and conflicts, rather than an objective expert, creates a new context for supervision. Within this relational context, the teach–treat boundary becomes less problematic. The supervisor's role may then safely extend somewhat beyond the didactic, as long as the supervisee's learning needs remain paramount. An illustrative vignette is offered.

Psychoanalysis and the Bilingual Patient

Some Observations on the Influence of Language Choice on the Transference

RoseMarie Foster PHD

RoseMarie Foster

RoseMarie Foster   Private practitioner in New York City and a Clinical Instructor of Psychiatry at NYU Medical Center. Her interests focus on various aspects of cross cultural psychology, including: the application of psychoanalytic theory and technique to non-European populations; the training of minority psychologists; and the investigation of how bilingualism impacts on the analytic process. Dr. Foster is currently working on an article about the role of language in the development of the self.

Bilingual patients, those whose mother tongue is not English, constitute a significant portion of patients referred to us in urban areas. These patients present a formidable challenge to English-speaking clinicians who must consider that bilingualism per se may impact on how the patient narrates his or her life story in the treatment process. Neurologic and psycholinguistic literatures suggest that some bilinguals maintain separate language systems. They do not simply have a duplex set of words to refer to experiences, but rather have alternative and not necessarily congruent language-specific experiential inner worlds.

This article selectively reviews the literature on bilingualism as it relates to psychoanalytic and psychotherapeutic work, concluding that the presence of dual language systems modifies fundamental aspects of the treatment process.

I present a case example proposing that when both languages are used in treatment, language switching can trigger powerful shifts in transference phenomena, as affective experiences and early object relations are uniquely revived in the language in which they were lived. I apply aspects of contemporary psychoanalytic thinking to the language acquisition process, advancing the idea that language acquisition is an object relational function.

Hermeneutics Versus Science in Psychoanalysis

A Rigorous Humanistic View

Adelbert Jenkins PHD

Adelbert Jenkins

Adelbert Jenkins   Associate Professor of Psychology and Director of Undergraduate Studies in the Psychology Department at New York University. His interests include exploring teleological themes in psychology, especially the implications of such topics for psychodynamic clinical psychology and for minority psychology. He is currently working on a second edition of his book, The Psychology of the Afro-American: A Humanistic Approach (Pergamon, 1982).

There is considerable current controversy among Freudian scholars about whether psychoanalysis is basically a hermeneutic discipline not amenable to scientific validation or a body of theory and practice that can and must be susceptible to such investigation. This debate stems partly from unresolved issues regarding certain diverging philosophic orientations in Freudian theory. Holt characterized these perspectives as differing images of the human being—“mechanistic” and “humanistic”—in Freud's thought. This article argues that Rychlak's “rigorous” humanistic notion of psychological agency can (a) clarify the implicitly humanistic philosophical position of proponents of hermeneutics in psychoanalysis (e.g., Schafer), and (b) help build bridges to those positions which advocate the scientific validation of psychoanalysis.

The Origin of the Symbiotic Wish

Thomas Horner PHD

Thomas Horner

Thomas Horner   Director of the Infancy and Early Childhood Clinic in the Department of Psychiatry and a senior supervisor and consultant at the University Center for the Child and the Family at the University of Michigan. His research and theoretical work have been primarily in the area of early infant development and adaptation.

The psychoanalytic concept of symbiosis needs to be revised in order to accommodate advances in self psychological theory, findings of developmental psychology, and the longstanding impetus in psychoanalysis to be biologically relevant. This article addresses symbiosis from an integrated physical and social biological perspective, and posits the formation of the symbiotic wish as the essential subjective condition driving the organism to seek object relationships.

An Interpersonal Perspective

The Analyst's Unwitting Participation in the Patient's Change

Irwin Hirsch PHD

Irwin Hirsch

Irwin Hirsch   Director of the Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis; Professor of Psychology and Supervisor, Adelphi University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; and is on the editorial board of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. Much of his writing and teaching focuses on themes related to the transference—countertransference matrix.

Harry Stack Sullivan was the American “psychoanalytic pioneer” who shifted the emphasis from drive discharge to interpersonal relations as the basic building block of human personality development. He is not unique, in that most deviation from the basic classical Freudian position has always rotated around that same issue (Greenberg & Mitchell, 1983). As more early psychoanalytic correspondence gets published, it is clear that Ferenczi, in particular, was a major forerunner of both interpersonal theory and the British school of object relations. However, it is evident that Jung, Adler, and Rank also broke with Freud around the fundamental question of the primacy of sexual and aggressive drives. In the more modern era, object relations theorists and self psychologists both pay significantly less attention to drives and more exclusive attention to the patients historical and current relationships with others and the internalization of those relational configurations (Mitchell, 1988).

Interpersonal psychoanalysis, for many, is associated with Sullivan and has probably been the most criticized or ignored of the modern developments in psychoanalysis. For one, Sullivan's interpersonal psychoanalysis is the only significant orientation which was not initiated by an analyst who was initially classically trained, and this lack of credential has always hurt his credibility as well as that of the interpersonal school in total. In addition, many have believed that his theory of development gives short shrift to the intrapsychic world of the individual and that Sullivan's only interest was in the observable relations among people. Some have construed him to be too positivistic, a variation of American behavioral psychology. This impression has some basis, though others have found clear reference to an internalized self in his writing (e.g., his concept of personifications). It is this concept that forms the basis for unconscious motivation.
